docs.title: Welcome to C Programming Notes 👋
docs.text: "The only way to learn a new programming language is by writing programs in it." — Dennis Ritchie (Creator of C)

docs.title: 📖 About This Documentation
docs.text:
This is a personal reference guide for C Programming and Data Structures & Algorithms (DSA). It covers everything from the basics of C syntax to advanced data structures and algorithms — written in a clean, beginner-friendly format with real examples and code snippets.

Whether you're just starting out or revising for interviews, this doc has you covered. 🚀

docs.title: 🔵 Basics
docs.text:
Core C language fundamentals every programmer must know.

Topic Description Data Types int, float, char, double, void and more Input & Output printf, scanf, format specifiers Control Flow if, else, switch, ternary operator Loops for, while, do-while, break, continue Functions Declaration, recursion, scope Pointers Memory addresses, pointer arithmetic Memory Management malloc, calloc, free, stack vs heap Strings Character arrays, string functions Structs & Unions Custom data types, typedef File I/O fopen, fread, fwrite, fclose Preprocessors & Macros #define, #include, #ifdef Bitwise Operators AND, OR, XOR, shifts

docs.title: 📊 Complexity Analysis
docs.text:
Understanding how efficient your code is.

Topic Description Big O, Big Θ, Big Ω Asymptotic notations explained Time & Space Complexity Analyzing algorithm efficiency

docs.title: ⚙️ Algorithms
docs.text:
Step-by-step problem solving techniques.

Topic Description Sorting Bubble, Merge, Quick, Heap sort Searching Linear, Binary search Recursion & Backtracking Recursive thinking, N-Queens Dynamic Programming Memoization, tabulation, classic problems Greedy Algorithms Activity selection, Huffman coding Graph Algorithms Dijkstra, BFS, DFS, Kruskal, Prim

docs.title: 🗃️ Data Structures
docs.text:
Building blocks for efficient programs.

Topic Description Arrays Static arrays, 2D arrays, operations Stack LIFO, push, pop, applications Queue FIFO, circular queue, deque Trees Binary tree, BST, traversals Heap Min/Max heap, heapify Graphs Adjacency list/matrix, traversal Linked List Singly, doubly, circular Hashing Hash tables, collision handling Trie Prefix trees, string search

docs.title: 🏆 Practice Problems
docs.text:
Test your knowledge with curated problems.

Level Description Easy 🟢 Fundamentals, basic logic Medium 🟡 Moderate DSA problems Hard 🔴 Advanced, interview-level problems

docs.title: 🚀 How to Use This Doc
docs.text:
• Use the left sidebar to navigate between topics
  • Use the search bar (top right) to quickly find anything
  • Use the Table of Contents on the right to jump within a page
  • Code blocks have a copy button — use it!

docs.title: 📬 Source
docs.text:
Getting Started

New to C? Start with Data Types and work your way through the Basics section before moving to DSA topics.

docs.title: Data Types 📚
docs.text:
What You'll Learn

  • ✅ All C data types with examples
  • ✅ Memory allocation and sizes
  • ✅ Type conversion and casting
  • ✅ Practical applications
  • ✅ Common mistakes to avoid

docs.title: 📖 Introduction
docs.text:
Data types are the foundation of C programming. They tell the compiler what type of data a variable can store and how much memory to allocate.

Think of it this way

Just like you have different containers for different things (bottles for water, boxes for books), C has different data types for different kinds of information! 🎯

graph TD
    A[C Data Types] --> B[Primary Types]
    A --> C[Derived Types]
    A --> D[User-Defined Types]

    B --> B1[int - Whole Numbers]
    B --> B2[char - Characters]
    B --> B3[float - Decimals]
    B --> B4[double - Large Decimals]
    B --> B5[void - No Value]

    C --> C1[Arrays]
    C --> C2[Pointers]
    C --> C3[Functions]

    D --> D1[Structures]
    D --> D2[Unions]
    D --> D3[Enumerations]

docs.title: 1️⃣ Integer Type (int)
docs.text:
Stores whole numbers (no decimals).

Real-Life Example

  • Age of a person: 25
  • Number of students: 50
  • Year: 2024

Basic Usage

#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    int age = 25;
    int students = 50;
    int year = 2024;

    printf("Age: %d years\n", age);
    printf("Students: %d\n", students);
    printf("Year: %d\n", year);

    return 0;
}

Output: 

Age: 25 years
Students: 50
Year: 2024

Integer Variants

short int 

short int population = 30000;
printf("Population: %hd\n", population);
- Size: 2 bytes - Range: -32,768 to 32,767 - Use when: You need smaller numbers

int 

int salary = 50000;
printf("Salary: %d\n", salary);
- Size: 4 bytes - Range: -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647 - Use when: Standard integer operations

long int 

long int distance = 384400000L; // Earth to Moon in meters
printf("Distance: %ld meters\n", distance);
- Size: 8 bytes - Range: Very large numbers - Use when: You need huge numbers

unsigned int 

unsigned int score = 1000000;
printf("Score: %u\n", score);
- Size: 4 bytes - Range: 0 to 4,294,967,295 (only positive) - Use when: You only need positive numbers

Memory Visualization

Memory Layout of int (4 bytes = 32 bits)

int age = 25;

┌─────────┬─────────┬─────────┬─────────┐
│ Byte 1  │ Byte 2  │ Byte 3  │ Byte 4  │
├─────────┼─────────┼─────────┼─────────┤
│00000000 │00000000 │00000000 │00011001 │ = 25 in binary
└─────────┴─────────┴─────────┴─────────┘
     ↑                              ↑
Most Significant Byte    Least Significant Byte

Practical Example: Calculator

#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    int num1, num2;
    int sum, difference, product, quotient;

    printf("Enter first number: ");
    scanf("%d", &num1);

    printf("Enter second number: ");
    scanf("%d", &num2);

    sum        = num1 + num2;
    difference = num1 - num2;
    product    = num1 * num2;
    quotient   = num1 / num2;

    printf("\n📊 Results:\n");
    printf("Sum:        %d\n", sum);
    printf("Difference: %d\n", difference);
    printf("Product:    %d\n", product);
    printf("Quotient:   %d\n", quotient);

    return 0;
}

Common Mistake

int a = 10;
int b = 3;
int result = a / b;  // result = 3 (not 3.33!)
Integer division truncates the decimal part!

docs.title: 2️⃣ Character Type (char)
docs.text:
Stores single characters.

Real-Life Example

  • Grade: 'A'
  • Gender: 'M' or 'F'
  • Choice: 'Y' or 'N'

Basic Usage

#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    char grade  = 'A';
    char gender = 'M';
    char choice = 'Y';

    printf("Grade: %c\n", grade);
    printf("Gender: %c\n", gender);
    printf("Choice: %c\n", choice);

    // Characters are actually stored as numbers (ASCII)
    printf("\nASCII value of 'A': %d\n", grade);

    return 0;
}

Output: 

Grade: A
Gender: M
Choice: Y

ASCII value of 'A': 65

ASCII Table (Common Characters)

Character ASCII Value Character ASCII Value '0' 48 'a' 97 '1' 49 'b' 98 '9' 57 'z' 122 'A' 65 ' ' (space) 32 'B' 66 '\n' (newline) 10 'Z' 90 '\0' (null) 0

Memory Visualization

Memory Layout of char (1 byte = 8 bits)

char grade = 'A';

┌──────────┐
│ 01000001 │ = 65 (ASCII value of 'A')
└──────────┘
  1 byte

Character Operations

#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    char letter = 'A';

    printf("Original: %c\n", letter);

    // Convert to lowercase (add 32 to ASCII)
    letter = letter + 32;
    printf("Lowercase: %c\n", letter);

    // Convert back to uppercase (subtract 32)
    letter = letter - 32;
    printf("Uppercase: %c\n", letter);

    // Next letter in alphabet
    letter = letter + 1;
    printf("Next letter: %c\n", letter);

    return 0;
}

Output: 

Original: A
Lowercase: a
Uppercase: A
Next letter: B

Practical Example: Grade System

#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    char grade;

    printf("Enter your grade (A/B/C/D/F): ");
    scanf(" %c", &grade);

    switch(grade) {
        case 'A': case 'a':
            printf("🌟 Excellent! (90-100)\n"); break;
        case 'B': case 'b':
            printf("👍 Good! (80-89)\n"); break;
        case 'C': case 'c':
            printf("😊 Average (70-79)\n"); break;
        case 'D': case 'd':
            printf("😐 Below Average (60-69)\n"); break;
        case 'F': case 'f':
            printf("😞 Fail (Below 60)\n"); break;
        default:
            printf("❌ Invalid grade!\n");
    }

    return 0;
}

Character vs String

  • 'A' is a character (single quotes)
  • "A" is a string (double quotes) — array of characters

docs.title: 3️⃣ Floating Point Type (float)
docs.text:
Stores decimal numbers with ~6-7 decimal places precision.

Real-Life Example

  • Temperature: 98.6°F
  • Price: $19.99
  • Distance: 5.8 km

Basic Usage

#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    float temperature = 98.6;
    float price       = 19.99;
    float distance    = 5.8;

    printf("Temperature: %.1f°F\n", temperature);
    printf("Price: $%.2f\n", price);
    printf("Distance: %.1f km\n", distance);

    return 0;
}

Format Specifiers

Specifier Output Example %f Default (6 decimals) 3.140000 %.2f 2 decimal places 3.14 %.0f No decimals 3 %e Scientific notation 3.140000e+00

Memory Visualization

Memory Layout of float (4 bytes = 32 bits)

float pi = 3.14;

┌──────┬──────────┬───────────────────────┐
│ Sign │ Exponent │       Mantissa        │
├──────┼──────────┼───────────────────────┤
│ 1bit │  8 bits  │        23 bits        │
└──────┴──────────┴───────────────────────┘
  ↑         ↑               ↑
+/- sign  Power of 2   Fractional part

Precision Limitation

float a = 0.1;
float b = 0.2;
float sum = a + b;
printf("%.20f\n", sum); // 0.30000001192092895508
Floating point numbers have precision limits!

docs.title: 4️⃣ Double Precision Type (double)
docs.text:
Stores decimal numbers with ~15-16 decimal places precision.

When to Use Double

  • Scientific calculations
  • Financial applications
  • When you need high precision
  • Large decimal numbers

Float vs Double Comparison

float (Single Precision) 

float pi = 3.141592653589793;
printf("Float: %.15f\n", pi);
// Output: 3.141592741012573
- Size: 4 bytes | Precision: ~6-7 digits | Range: ±3.4 × 10³⁸

double (Double Precision) 

double pi = 3.141592653589793;
printf("Double: %.15lf\n", pi);
// Output: 3.141592653589793
- Size: 8 bytes | Precision: ~15-16 digits | Range: ±1.7 × 10³⁰⁸

long double 

long double pi = 3.141592653589793238L;
printf("Long: %.18Lf\n", pi);
- Size: 16 bytes | Precision: ~19 digits | Range: Largest

Practical Example: Circle Calculator

#include <stdio.h>
#include <math.h>

int main() {
    double radius, area, circumference;
    const double PI = 3.141592653589793;

    printf("Enter circle radius: ");
    scanf("%lf", &radius);

    area          = PI * radius * radius;
    circumference = 2 * PI * radius;

    printf("\n🔵 Circle Properties:\n");
    printf("Radius:        %.2lf units\n", radius);
    printf("Area:          %.2lf square units\n", area);
    printf("Circumference: %.2lf units\n", circumference);

    return 0;
}

Rule of Thumb

  • Use float for graphics/games (speed matters)
  • Use double for scientific/financial calculations (precision matters)

docs.title: 5️⃣ Void Type (void)
docs.text:
Represents "no type" or "no value".

Three Main Uses

  1. Functions that don't return a value
  2. Functions with no parameters
  3. Generic pointers

Use Case 1: Functions with No Return

#include <stdio.h>

void greet() {
    printf("Hello, World! 👋\n");
}

void printLine() {
    printf("═══════════════════════\n");
}

int main() {
    printLine();
    greet();
    printLine();
    return 0;
}

Use Case 2: Generic Pointers

#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    int   num  = 10;
    float fnum = 3.14;

    void *ptr;  // Generic pointer

    ptr = #
    printf("Integer: %d\n", *(int*)ptr);

    ptr = &fnum;
    printf("Float: %.2f\n", *(float*)ptr);

    return 0;
}

Important

You cannot create a variable of type void: 

void x;  // ❌ ERROR!

docs.title: 📊 Complete Size and Range Table
docs.text:
#include <stdio.h>
#include <limits.h>
#include <float.h>

int main() {
    printf("%-20s %-10s %s\n", "Data Type", "Size", "Range");
    printf("%-20s %-10s %s\n", "─────────────────", "────────", "──────────────────────");
    printf("%-20s %-10lu %d to %d\n",    "char",           sizeof(char),           CHAR_MIN,  CHAR_MAX);
    printf("%-20s %-10lu %d to %d\n",    "unsigned char",  sizeof(unsigned char),  0,         UCHAR_MAX);
    printf("%-20s %-10lu %d to %d\n",    "short int",      sizeof(short),          SHRT_MIN,  SHRT_MAX);
    printf("%-20s %-10lu %d to %d\n",    "int",            sizeof(int),            INT_MIN,   INT_MAX);
    printf("%-20s %-10lu %u\n",          "unsigned int",   sizeof(unsigned int),   UINT_MAX);
    printf("%-20s %-10lu Very large\n",  "long int",       sizeof(long));
    printf("%-20s %-10lu %.2e to %.2e\n","float",          sizeof(float),          FLT_MIN,   FLT_MAX);
    printf("%-20s %-10lu %.2e to %.2e\n","double",         sizeof(double),         DBL_MIN,   DBL_MAX);
    return 0;
}

Visual Size Comparison

graph LR
    A[char\n1 byte] --> B[short\n2 bytes]
    B --> C[int\n4 bytes]
    C --> D[float\n4 bytes]
    D --> E[long\n8 bytes]
    E --> F[double\n8 bytes]
    F --> G[long double\n16 bytes]

docs.title: 1️⃣ Arrays
docs.text:
Collection of elements of the same type stored in contiguous memory.

Memory Layout

int marks[5] = {85, 90, 78, 92, 88};

Index:   [0]   [1]   [2]   [3]   [4]
       ┌─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┐
Value: │ 85  │ 90  │ 78  │ 92  │ 88  │
       └─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┘
Addr:  1000  1004  1008  1012  1016

Total Memory: 5 × 4 = 20 bytes

Single-Dimensional Array

#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    int marks[5] = {85, 90, 78, 92, 88};

    printf("📊 Student Marks:\n");
    printf("─────────────────\n");

    for(int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        printf("Subject %d: %d\n", i+1, marks[i]);
    }

    int sum = 0;
    for(int i = 0; i < 5; i++) sum += marks[i];
    float average = sum / 5.0;

    printf("─────────────────\n");
    printf("Average: %.2f\n", average);

    return 0;
}

Multi-Dimensional Array

#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    int matrix[3][3] = {
        {1, 2, 3},
        {4, 5, 6},
        {7, 8, 9}
    };

    printf("📐 Matrix:\n");
    for(int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
        for(int j = 0; j < 3; j++) {
            printf("%d ", matrix[i][j]);
        }
        printf("\n");
    }
    return 0;
}

Array Index

Arrays start at index 0, not 1! 

int arr[5];
// Valid:   arr[0], arr[1], arr[2], arr[3], arr[4]
// Invalid: arr[5]  ❌ (out of bounds!)

docs.title: 2️⃣ Pointers
docs.text:
Variables that store memory addresses.

Why Pointers?

  • Dynamic memory allocation
  • Efficient array/string handling
  • Pass by reference
  • Data structures (linked lists, trees)

Basic Pointer Concepts

#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    int num  = 42;
    int *ptr = #  // Store address of num

    printf("Value of num:      %d\n",   num);
    printf("Address of num:    %p\n",   (void*)&num);
    printf("Value of ptr:      %p\n",   (void*)ptr);
    printf("Value at address:  %d\n",   *ptr);

    // Modify through pointer
    *ptr = 100;
    printf("\nAfter *ptr = 100:\n");
    printf("Value of num:      %d\n", num);

    return 0;
}

Visual Representation

    num              ptr
  ┌─────┐          ┌──────┐
  │ 42  │          │ 1000 │ ──── points to num
  └─────┘          └──────┘
  Addr: 1000       Addr: 2000

& (address-of): &num  → returns 1000
* (dereference): *ptr → returns value at address (42)

Practical Example: Swap with Pointers

Without Pointers ❌ 
void swap(int a, int b) {
    int temp = a;
    a = b;
    b = temp;
}
// Output: x=10, y=20 (NOT swapped!)
With Pointers ✅ 
void swap(int *a, int *b) {
    int temp = *a;
    *a = *b;
    *b = temp;
}
// Output: x=20, y=10 (swapped!)

Pointer Best Practices

int *ptr = NULL;     // Always initialize to NULL

if(ptr != NULL) {    // Always check before use
    // Safe to use ptr
}

docs.title: 3️⃣ Functions
docs.text:
Reusable blocks of code that perform specific tasks.

Function Types

No Params, No Return 
void greet(void) {
    printf("Hello!\n");
}
With Params, No Return 
void printSquare(int num) {
    printf("%d² = %d\n", num, num*num);
}
No Params, With Return 
int getNumber(void) {
    return 42;
}
With Params and Return 
int multiply(int a, int b) {
    return a * b;
}

Recursion Example: Factorial

#include <stdio.h>

int factorial(int n) {
    if(n == 0 || n == 1) return 1;     // Base case
    return n * factorial(n - 1);       // Recursive case
}

int main() {
    printf("5! = %d\n", factorial(5)); // Output: 120
    return 0;
}

graph TD
    A[factorial 5] --> B[5 × factorial 4]
    B --> C[4 × factorial 3]
    C --> D[3 × factorial 2]
    D --> E[2 × factorial 1]
    E --> F[returns 1]
    F -.->|1| E
    E -.->|2| D
    D -.->|6| C
    C -.->|24| B
    B -.->|120| A

docs.title: Structures (struct)
docs.text:
Groups different data types under one name.

#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

struct Student {
    char name[50];
    int  age;
    float gpa;
};

int main() {
    struct Student s1;
    strcpy(s1.name, "Alice");
    s1.age = 20;
    s1.gpa = 3.8;

    printf("Name: %s\n",  s1.name);
    printf("Age:  %d\n",  s1.age);
    printf("GPA:  %.1f\n", s1.gpa);

    return 0;
}

docs.title: Unions
docs.text:
Like structs but all members share the same memory.

union Data {
    int   i;
    float f;
    char  str[20];
};
// All members share the same memory location

docs.title: Enumerations (enum)
docs.text:
Assigns names to integer constants.

enum Day { MON=1, TUE, WED, THU, FRI, SAT, SUN };

int main() {
    enum Day today = WED;
    printf("Day number: %d\n", today); // Output: 3
    return 0;
}

docs.title: Implicit (Automatic)
docs.text:
int   i = 10;
float f = i;    // int → float automatically
printf("%f\n", f); // 10.000000

docs.title: Explicit (Casting)
docs.text:
float pi     = 3.14159;
int   approx = (int)pi;   // Explicit cast: float → int
printf("%d\n", approx);   // Output: 3

Data Loss in Casting

float f = 9.99;
int   i = (int)f;  // i = 9 (decimal part lost!)

docs.title: ✅ Quick Reference Summary
docs.text: Type Size Format Example Value char 1 byte %c 'A' int 4 bytes %d 42 float 4 bytes %f 3.14 double 8 bytes %lf 3.141592653589 long 8 bytes %ld 1234567890L short 2 bytes %hd 32767 unsigned 4 bytes %u 4294967295

docs.title: Big O, Big Θ, Big Ω
